sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 12 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 171, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Initial state - Level 1. Need to understand the board layout and goal.
- Goal model: Unknown yet - need to explore the board structure.
- Plan: First, inspect the segmentation to understand objects on the board.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 170 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 2.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
All 12 LEFT actions were no_impact (only HUD changed). The player didn't move at all! Looking at the image, the player is still at the bottom-right corner. This means the player is constrained and cannot move left from the current position.
Wait, but earlier I moved LEFT from the bottom-right and the player did move left. Let me check the current player position.
Looking at the image, the player appears to be at the bottom-right corner. The segmentation showed the player at cols 56-59 after the SPACE action. Now after 12 LEFT actions, the player should still be at cols 56-59 (since all were no_impact).
But wait, `gameplay_changed: false` and `no_impact: true` for all 12 actions. This means the player DIDN'T move. The player is stuck at the current position.
Why can't the player move left? Maybe the player is constrained to a specific region. Or maybe there's a wall/blocker preventing left movement.
Looking at the image more carefully, I see the player at the bottom-right corner. The bottom edge has a pink bar on the left and a dark bar on the right. Maybe the player is constrained to the dark bar region?
Actually, looking at the initial state, the player was at the bottom center. After collecting the first target, I moved RIGHT 16 times and the player moved to the bottom-right. Then I moved LEFT and the player moved left, but only a few times before hitting a boundary.
Wait, let me reconsider. The `no_impact` result means the action only changed the HUD band, not the gameplay. But the player IS part of the gameplay. If the player didn't move, then the action was no_impact.
So the player is stuck at the bottom-right corner and can't move left. This is very strange.
Let me check the current segmentation to see where the player actually is.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'N', 'c', 'G']:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The player structure has changed significantly:
- Node 7: color=c (charcoal), pixels=12, boundary=[[56, 56], [56, 59], [59, 59], [59, 56]] - this is a 4x4 charcoal frame at rows 56-59, cols 56-59
- Node 9: color=W (white), pixels=3, boundary=[[60, 8], [62, 8]] - this is a vertical 3x1 white strip at rows 60-62, col 8
- Node 10: color=N (light green), pixels=9, boundary=[[60, 9], [60, 11], [62, 11], [62, 9]] - this is a 3x3 green body at rows 60-62, cols 9-11
Wait, the white strip (node 9) is at col 8, and the green body (node 10) is at cols 9-11. These are NOT connected! The white strip is at the far left (col 8) and the green body is at cols 9-11, which is also on the left side.
But node 7 (charcoal frame) is at cols 56-59, which is on the right side. This doesn't look like a coherent player structure anymore.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. Maybe:
- Node 7 is NOT the player frame - it's something else (a target or obstacle)
- Node 9 (white strip) and Node 10 (green body) are the player, now at the bottom-left
Looking at the image, I can see:
- A small blue square at the bottom-right (this might be the charcoal frame node 7, but it looks blue in the image)
- A white/green player at the bottom-left
So the player moved from the bottom-right to the bottom-left! But I pressed LEFT 12 times and all were no_impact. How did the player move?
Wait, maybe the LEFT actions DID move the player, but the no_impact check was wrong because the HUD band overlaps with the player. Or maybe the player wrapped around from right to left?
Actually, looking more carefully at the image:
- Bottom-left: white strip + green body (the player)
- Bottom-right: a small blue/charcoal square (node 7)
The player is now at the bottom-left, near cols 8-11. The left target is at cols 16-19. So the player is close to the left target!
Let me check: the left target is at rows 28-31, cols 16-19. The player is at rows 60-62, cols 8-11. To collect the left target, I need to move the player to rows 28-31 (directly below the target) and cols 16-19.
But wait, the player seems to have moved from the bottom-right to the bottom-left in one action (the last SPACE action). This suggests SPACE might teleport the player to a different position.
Let me reconsider the SPACE mechanic:
1. First SPACE (initial state): collected the center target, toggled frame color
2. Second SPACE: toggled frame color, moved player from cols 44-47 to cols 56-59
3. Third SPACE (implicit in the LEFT batch? No, I didn't press SPACE in the LEFT batch)
Wait, I only pressed SPACE once after the initial state. The LEFT batch didn't include SPACE. So how did the player move from cols 56-59 to cols 8-11?
Actually, looking at the segmentation again:
- Node 7: color=c, boundary=[[56, 56], [56, 59], [59, 59], [59, 56]] - this is at cols 56-59, which is where the player was before
- Node 9: color=W, boundary=[[60, 8], [62, 8]] - white strip at col 8
- Node 10: color=N, boundary=[[60, 9], [60, 11], [62, 11], [62, 9]] - green body at cols 9-11
Maybe node 7 is a remnant of the player's old position (a "ghost" or trail), and nodes 9+10 are the actual player at the new position.
But I didn't press any action that would move the player from right to left. The LEFT actions were all no_impact. So the player shouldn't have moved.
Unless... the no_impact check is wrong, and the LEFT actions DID move the player. The player moved from cols 56-59 all the way to cols 8-11, which is 48 columns left. But I only pressed LEFT 12 times. So the player moved 4 columns per LEFT action? That seems unlikely.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the player wrapped around: from col 56, moving LEFT 12 times would go to col 44. But the player is now at col 8-11. That doesn't match wrapping either.
I'm confused. Let me just look at the current state and try to make progress. The player is at the bottom-left (cols 8-11), and the left target is at cols 16-19. I need to move RIGHT to align with the target, then move UP to be directly below it, then press SPACE.
But wait, looking at the image, the player appears to be at the bottom-left edge. The left target is at cols 16-19, which is to the right of the player. Let me try moving RIGHT to align with the target.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player appears to be at bottom-left now (cols 8-11)
# Left target at cols 16-19
# Need RIGHT to align: from col 8 to col 16 = 8 RIGHT moves
# But first let me verify the player can move RIGHT
action(['RIGHT'] * 8, intent='solve')
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
